text:
# Page 1^page=1
My First Sourdough Starter Workbook Making sourdough starter is like planting a garden with wild yeast. Yeast exists everywhere. On fruit, in the air and in the flour. You don’t need to do anything special to get yeast, it’s already in the flour. Think of the yeast as our plants, the flour and water is the food and temperature is the sun. Our yeast garden needs all of these things to grow big and strong so it can make us yummy loaves of bread someday. Harvest your yeast too soon and it will be too weak so it’s important to give it one week to grow before attempt- ing to make bread. Yeast is very hard to kill but it will be erratic at first. From day to day it will look huge, then not grow at all. It will be thick and other times it might look like water. These are all normal stages. Follow through with all the steps in this workbook and you’ll have some beautiful starter by the end of the week. I have also included some tips and tricks along the way if your starter is truly acting up. Date Started Time Started

# Page 2^page=2
Sourdough Starter Recipe Some notes before you begin. Using whole wheat flour is preferred because it has more microbial activity and yeast than white flour. Making this process very easy. You can also use all-purpose flour or bread flour as long as it’s unbleached. Bleach and yeast do not do well together. Using bottled or filtered water is idea for starting your sourdough starter. Tap water contains chlorine and impu- rities that can get in the way of the yeast growing. Use a clean and sanitized jar to grow your starter. I ran my jar through the dishwasher because it has a high heat sanitize setting. You can also boil the jar then let it air dry before using it. Use a wooden spoon to stir your starter after the first day. Yeast is very acidic and can break down metal and caust it to leach into your starter. Remember that sourdough starter needs warmth. The top of your fridge is a great place for it to hang out or in- side your oven with the light turned on but don’t forget that it’s in there! We will be feeding in 24 hour increments at first them move to 12 hour feedings so it’s ideal to start your sour- dough starter in the morning so that when we move to 12 hour feedings you aren’t scheduled to feed in the middle of the night. I started mine at 8am.

# Page 3^page=3
Day 1 Day 1 - Make Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 150 grams whole wheat flour 150 grams bottled or filtered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Combine together with your hand in a small bowl until no raw flour remains. 2. Transfer the starter to a clean, straight sided container like a glass jar. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. What is the temperature of the room, what ingredients did you use if not listed above. Did you make any changes? Where did you store the starter. Did you use warm water? What does it look like after 24 hours?

# Page 4^page=4
Day 2 Day 2 - Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 75 grams whole wheat flour 75 grams bottled or filtered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Remove half the starter (you can eyeball this) and throw it away. We can’t use it for anything yet. 2. Add 75grams whole wheat flour and 75 grams warm bottled or filtered water and stir until no flour remains. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. You may see some bubbles today or you might not see anything at all. Make note of what you see below. What is the temperature of the room? How tall did your starter rise? Did you see a lot of activity or none at all? What does it smell like?

# Page 5^page=5
Day 3 Day 3 - Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 75 grams whole wheat flour 75 grams bottled or filtered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Remove half the starter (you can eyeball this) and throw it away. We can’t use it for anything yet. 2. Add 75grams whole wheat flour and 75 grams warm bottled or filtered water and stir until no flour remains. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. You may see some bubbles today or you might not see anything at all. Make note of what you see below. What is the temperature of the room? How tall did your starter rise? Did you see a lot of activity or none at all? What does it smell like?

# Page 6^page=6
Day 4 Day 4 - Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 75 grams whole wheat flour 75 grams bottled or filtered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Remove half the starter (you can eyeball this) and throw it away. We can’t use it for anything yet. 2. Add 75grams whole wheat flour and 75 grams warm bottled or filtered water and stir until no flour remains. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. You may see some bubbles today or you might not see anything at all. Make note of what you see below. What is the temperature of the room? How tall did your starter rise? Did you see a lot of activity or none at all? What does it smell like?

# Page 7^page=7
Day 5 Day 5 - Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 75 grams unbleached bread or all-purpose fl our 75 grams bottled or fi ltered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Remove half the starter (you can eyeball this) make sourdough pikelets! 2. Add 75grams unbleached bread or AP fl our and 75 grams water (90ºF) and stir until no fl our remains. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. 5. Repeat this process 12 hours later. Set an alarm so you don’t forget. Today we are switching to twice a day feedings and changing to bread or unbleached all-purpose fl our! Once in the morning and once at night. Activity levels should be increasing. Don’t worry if there isn’t a strong sour smell. What is the temperature of the room? How tall did your starter rise? Did you see a lot of activity or none at all? What does it smell like? If you cooked your discard, how did it taste?

# Page 8^page=8
Sourdough Starter Pikelets Ingredients Instructions 1 Cup Sourdough discard (roughly) 1 teaspoon sugar 1/4 teaspoon salt 1/4 teaspoon baking soda 1 Tabespoon butter Combine together all your ingredients with a fork. The mixure will look thick and puff up a bit. Heat a pan on medium heat for 5 minutes then add 1 Tablespoon of butter to coat the bottom of the pan. I use my hand but you can use a spoon to divide the batter into three equal sized pieces or do one huge one! Cook for 3-4 minutes until the edges start to look dry and you can see the bubbles popping on top. Flip the pikelet over and continue cooking for another 3 minutes. Slather in more butter and enjoy! It might be too early to make sourdough but that doesn’t mean you can’t enjoy the fruits of your labor so far! These pikelets (similar to a pancake) are the perfect use for your leftover starter and make an amazing breakfast! You can make about three pikelets from your dis- card. Try mixing in some chopped chives or bacon! SO delish!

# Page 9^page=9
Day 6 Day 6 - Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 75 grams unbleached bread or all-purpose fl our 75 grams bottled or fi ltered water warmed to 90ºF 1. Remove half the starter (you can eyeball this) make sourdough pikelets! 2. Add 75grams unbleached bread or AP fl our and 75 grams water (90ºF) and stir until no fl our remains. 3. Cover with plastic wrap loosely and set in a warm area like on top of your fridge. 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. 5. Repeat this process 12 hours later. Set an alarm so you don’t forget. You may be seeing tons of activity and lots of bubbles and think you’re ready to bake bread but right now our starter is a wild beast that isn’t very reliable. While you COULD try to make a loaf, it may not rise because the starter isn’t very strong. What is the temperature of the room? How tall did your starter rise? Did you see a lot of activity or none at all? What does it smell like? If you cooked your discard, how did it taste?

# Page 10^page=10
Day 7 Day 7- Feed Starter 24 Hours Later Ingredients Instructions Notes 150 grams unbleached bread or all-purpose fl our (for a more sour starter, use 75 grams) 150 grams bottled or fi ltered water warmed to 90ºF (for a more sour starter, use 75 grams) 1. Remove all but 75 grams of your starter. 2. Add 150 grams unbleached all-purpose fl our or bread fl our and 150 grams bottled water at room temperature. 3. Transfer to a clean jar and name your starter! 4. Mark a line or place a piece of tape to mark the level of the starter. Make note every hour how much it rises and keep track of when it “peaks” and begins to fall again. Repeat this process of feeding every time you feed your starter (once ever 24 hours) Congratulations! Your starter should be strong enough to use in sourdough recipes! It should rise and fall reliably. From now on you will only feed it once per day. Always equal parts starter, fl our and water. If you want to make bread, keep in mind you will use your starter to make a levain. To make a levain you take some of your starter before you feed it, add it to equal parts fl our and water (as you do when you feed) and then let it rise to it’s peak of activity. Usually between 2-5 hours depending on the warmth of your area. My levain is 50 grams starter, 50 grams whole wheat fl our and 50 grams warm bottled water 90ºF. Most bread recipes will specify how much levain to make for the recipe. Be sure to check out my Sourdough Bread Recipe For Beginners Post! www.sugargeekshow.com
